description: Aggregated index of all component intents across BaseX UI. Used by the MCP server and AI agents to resolve developer intent to component recommendations.

intents.reasoning: Button is the primary component for triggering user actions. Use for any clickable action that is not navigation.
Confirmation actions use the default solid button to indicate the primary positive action.
Cancel actions use outline or ghost to de-emphasize relative to the primary action.
Destructive actions use the destructive color to warn users of irreversible consequences.
Secondary actions use outline or ghost variant to visually de-emphasize them relative to the primary action.
Accordion is the primary component for organizing content into collapsible sections. Each section has a trigger heading that reveals its content panel.
Accordion provides progressive disclosure — content is hidden by default and revealed on demand, reducing cognitive load.
intents.composition:
<Accordion.Root>
  <Accordion.Item value="details">
    <Accordion.Header>
      <Accordion.Trigger>Show details</Accordion.Trigger>
    </Accordion.Header>
    <Accordion.Panel>Hidden content revealed on click</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
</Accordion.Root>

intents.reasoning: FAQ lists are a classic accordion pattern — each question is a trigger, each answer is a panel. Use single-open mode to keep focus.
intents.composition:
<Accordion.Root>
  <Accordion.Item value="q1">
    <Accordion.Header>
      <Accordion.Trigger>How do I reset my password?</Accordion.Trigger>
    </Accordion.Header>
    <Accordion.Panel>Visit the settings page and click "Reset Password".</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
</Accordion.Root>

intents.reasoning: AlertDialog is the correct component when a destructive or irreversible action requires explicit user confirmation before proceeding.
intents.composition:
<AlertDialog.Root>
  <AlertDialog.Trigger render={<Button variant="outline" color="destructive">Delete</Button>} />
  <AlertDialog.Portal>
    <AlertDialog.Backdrop />
    <AlertDialog.Popup>
      <AlertDialog.Title>Delete this item?</AlertDialog.Title>
      <AlertDialog.Description>This action cannot be undone.</AlertDialog.Description>
      <AlertDialog.Actions>
        <AlertDialog.Close render={<Button variant="ghost">Cancel</Button>} />
        <AlertDialog.Close render={<Button color="destructive">Delete</Button>} onClick={handleDelete} />
      </AlertDialog.Actions>
    </AlertDialog.Popup>
  </AlertDialog.Portal>
</AlertDialog.Root>

intents.reasoning: AlertDialog blocks interaction until the user explicitly acknowledges or confirms. Use when skipping or ignoring the prompt is not acceptable.
intents.composition:
<AlertDialog.Root>
  <AlertDialog.Trigger render={<Button>Continue</Button>} />
  <AlertDialog.Portal>
    <AlertDialog.Backdrop />
    <AlertDialog.Popup>
      <AlertDialog.Title>Accept terms?</AlertDialog.Title>
      <AlertDialog.Description>You must accept before continuing.</AlertDialog.Description>
      <AlertDialog.Actions>
        <AlertDialog.Close render={<Button variant="ghost">Cancel</Button>} />
        <AlertDialog.Close render={<Button>Accept</Button>} onClick={handleAccept} />
      </AlertDialog.Actions>
    </AlertDialog.Popup>
  </AlertDialog.Portal>
</AlertDialog.Root>

intents.reasoning: AlertDialog prevents accidental data loss by forcing the user to confirm before discarding unsaved work.
intents.composition:
<AlertDialog.Root>
  <AlertDialog.Trigger render={<Button variant="ghost">Leave</Button>} />
  <AlertDialog.Portal>
    <AlertDialog.Backdrop />
    <AlertDialog.Popup>
      <AlertDialog.Title>Discard changes?</AlertDialog.Title>
      <AlertDialog.Description>You have unsaved changes that will be lost.</AlertDialog.Description>
      <AlertDialog.Actions>
        <AlertDialog.Close render={<Button variant="ghost">Keep editing</Button>} />
        <AlertDialog.Close render={<Button color="destructive">Discard</Button>} onClick={handleDiscard} />
      </AlertDialog.Actions>
    </AlertDialog.Popup>
  </AlertDialog.Portal>
</AlertDialog.Root>

intents.reasoning: Dialog is the right component for displaying focused informational content in an overlay that the user can easily dismiss.
intents.composition:
<Dialog.Root>
  <Dialog.Trigger render={<Button variant="outline">View details</Button>} />
  <Dialog.Portal>
    <Dialog.Backdrop />
    <Dialog.Popup>
      <Dialog.Header>
        <Dialog.Title>Item details</Dialog.Title>
        <Dialog.Description>Detail content here.</Dialog.Description>
      </Dialog.Header>
      <Dialog.Panel>
        {/* detail content */}
      </Dialog.Panel>
      <Dialog.Footer>
        <Dialog.Close render={<Button variant="ghost">Close</Button>} />
      </Dialog.Footer>
    </Dialog.Popup>
  </Dialog.Portal>
</Dialog.Root>

intents.reasoning: Dialog provides modal focus for form interactions, preventing accidental clicks outside while the user is editing.
intents.composition:
<Dialog.Root open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
  <Dialog.Trigger render={<Button>Edit</Button>} />
  <Dialog.Portal>
    <Dialog.Backdrop />
    <Dialog.Popup>
      <form onSubmit={handleSubmit} style={{ display: 'contents' }}>
        <Dialog.Header>
          <Dialog.Title>Edit item</Dialog.Title>
          <Dialog.Description>Update the fields below.</Dialog.Description>
        </Dialog.Header>
        <Dialog.Panel>
          {/* form fields */}
        </Dialog.Panel>
        <Dialog.Footer>
          <Dialog.Close render={<Button variant="ghost">Cancel</Button>} />
          <Button type="submit">Save</Button>
        </Dialog.Footer>
      </form>
    </Dialog.Popup>
  </Dialog.Portal>
</Dialog.Root>

intents.reasoning: Dialog works well for focused settings interactions that benefit from modal isolation without the forced-acknowledgment behaviour of AlertDialog.
intents.composition:
<Dialog.Root>
  <Dialog.Trigger render={<Button variant="outline">Settings</Button>} />
  <Dialog.Portal>
    <Dialog.Backdrop />
    <Dialog.Popup>
      <Dialog.Header>
        <Dialog.Title>Settings</Dialog.Title>
        <Dialog.Description>Configure your preferences.</Dialog.Description>
      </Dialog.Header>
      <Dialog.Panel>
        {/* settings controls */}
      </Dialog.Panel>
      <Dialog.Footer>
        <Dialog.Close render={<Button>Done</Button>} />
      </Dialog.Footer>
    </Dialog.Popup>
  </Dialog.Portal>
</Dialog.Root>

intents.reasoning: Avatar displays a user's photo with automatic fallback to initials when the image is unavailable.
Avatar Fallback renders initials on a muted background when no image is available.
Avatar Fallback can render an icon SVG as a generic placeholder for anonymous or system users.
Checkbox is the standard control for toggling a boolean option that is saved with a form.
A required checkbox is the standard UX pattern for explicit consent before form submission.
intents.composition:
<label>
  <Checkbox.Root required>
    <Checkbox.Indicator />
  </Checkbox.Root>
  I agree to the terms and conditions
</label>

intents.reasoning: Multiple checkboxes let users select several items from a list. Combine with an indeterminate parent for 'select all'.
CheckboxGroup manages shared state across multiple checkboxes, tracking which items are selected via a value array.
intents.composition:
<CheckboxGroup.Root defaultValue={['a']}>
  <label>
    <Checkbox.Root name="a">
      <Checkbox.Indicator />
    </Checkbox.Root>
    Option A
  </label>
  <label>
    <Checkbox.Root name="b">
      <Checkbox.Indicator />
    </Checkbox.Root>
    Option B
  </label>
</CheckboxGroup.Root>

intents.reasoning: A parent checkbox inside a controlled CheckboxGroup auto-derives its checked/indeterminate state from children, providing a native select-all pattern. The group MUST be controlled (value + onValueChange) for the parent checkbox to work.
intents.composition:
const [value, setValue] = useState<string[]>([]);

<CheckboxGroup.Root allValues={['a', 'b', 'c']} value={value} onValueChange={setValue}>
  <label>
    <Checkbox.Root parent>
      <Checkbox.Indicator />
    </Checkbox.Root>
    Select all
  </label>
  <label>
    <Checkbox.Root name="a">
      <Checkbox.Indicator />
    </Checkbox.Root>
    Option A
  </label>
</CheckboxGroup.Root>

intents.reasoning: Collapsible is the primary component for a single collapsible section with a trigger and animated panel.
Collapsible provides simple progressive disclosure — content is hidden by default and revealed on demand with an animated panel.
Autocomplete is the primary component for text inputs that show filtered suggestions as the user types.
intents.composition:
<Autocomplete.Root items={items}>
  <Autocomplete.Input placeholder="Search..." />
  <Autocomplete.Popup>
    <Autocomplete.Empty>No results found.</Autocomplete.Empty>
    {(item) => (
      <Autocomplete.Item key={item.id} value={item}>
        {item.value}
      </Autocomplete.Item>
    )}
  </Autocomplete.Popup>
</Autocomplete.Root>

intents.reasoning: Autocomplete provides the text-input-with-filtered-list pattern used in command palettes. Combine with a Dialog for the overlay.
intents.composition:
<Autocomplete.Root items={commands}>
  <Autocomplete.Input placeholder="Type a command..." />
  <Autocomplete.Popup>
    <Autocomplete.Empty>No commands found.</Autocomplete.Empty>
    {(cmd) => (
      <Autocomplete.Item key={cmd.id} value={cmd}>
        {cmd.label}
      </Autocomplete.Item>
    )}
  </Autocomplete.Popup>
</Autocomplete.Root>

intents.reasoning: Combobox is the primary component for selecting from a predefined list with the ability to search/filter options.
intents.composition:
<Combobox.Root items={items}>
  <Combobox.Input placeholder="Select..." />
  <Combobox.Popup>
    <Combobox.Empty>No results found.</Combobox.Empty>
    {(item) => (
      <Combobox.Item key={item.id} value={item}>
        <Combobox.ItemIndicator />
        {item.value}
      </Combobox.Item>
    )}
  </Combobox.Popup>
</Combobox.Root>

intents.reasoning: Combobox provides a dropdown that filters options as the user types, ideal for long option lists.
Combobox with multiple mode allows selecting several items from a list with checkmark indicators.
intents.composition:
<Combobox.Root items={items} multiple>
  <Combobox.Input placeholder="Select items..." />
  <Combobox.Popup>
    <Combobox.Empty>No results.</Combobox.Empty>
    {(item) => (
      <Combobox.Item key={item.id} value={item}>
        <Combobox.ItemIndicator />
        {item.value}
      </Combobox.Item>
    )}
  </Combobox.Popup>
</Combobox.Root>

intents.reasoning: Autocomplete works well for searching through and selecting from a list of tags or labels.
intents.composition:
<Autocomplete.Root items={tags}>
  <Autocomplete.Input placeholder="Search tags..." />
  <Autocomplete.Popup>
    <Autocomplete.Empty>No tags found.</Autocomplete.Empty>
    {(tag) => (
      <Autocomplete.Item key={tag.id} value={tag}>
        {tag.value}
      </Autocomplete.Item>
    )}
  </Autocomplete.Popup>
</Autocomplete.Root>

intents.reasoning: Drawer provides a slide-out panel anchored to a screen edge, ideal for navigation menus on mobile or supplementary content.
Drawer with default bottom swipe direction provides a mobile-native bottom sheet pattern with swipe-to-dismiss.
Drawer provides a slide-out panel for forms that don't need the full-screen blocking of a Dialog.
intents.composition:
<Drawer.Root>
  <Drawer.Trigger render={<Button>Add item</Button>} />
  <Drawer.Portal>
    <Drawer.Backdrop />
    <Drawer.Popup>
      <form onSubmit={handleSubmit} style={{ display: 'contents' }}>
        <Drawer.Header>
          <Drawer.Title>Add item</Drawer.Title>
        </Drawer.Header>
        <Drawer.Panel>
          {/* form fields */}
        </Drawer.Panel>
        <Drawer.Footer>
          <Drawer.Close render={<Button variant="ghost">Cancel</Button>} />
          <Button type="submit">Save</Button>
        </Drawer.Footer>
      </form>
    </Drawer.Popup>
  </Drawer.Portal>
</Drawer.Root>

intents.reasoning: Field connects a label, description, input control, and validation error with proper accessibility attributes.
Field automatically connects validation errors to the input control for screen readers via aria-describedby.
intents.composition:
<Field.Root>
  <Field.Label>Email</Field.Label>
  <Field.Control type="email" required />
  <Field.Error match="typeMismatch">Please enter a valid email.</Field.Error>
  <Field.Error match="valueMissing">Email is required.</Field.Error>
</Field.Root>

intents.reasoning: Fieldset semantically groups related form controls with an accessible legend, essential for screen readers.
Fieldset's disabled prop cascades to all child form controls, providing a single switch to disable an entire group.
intents.composition:
<Fieldset.Root disabled>
  <Fieldset.Legend>Locked section</Fieldset.Legend>
  {/* All fields within are disabled */}
</Fieldset.Root>

intents.reasoning: Form manages server-side validation errors and distributes them to child Field components automatically.
intents.composition:
<Form errors={serverErrors} onClearErrors={handleClearErrors} onSubmit={handleSubmit}>
  <Field.Root name="email">
    <Field.Label>Email</Field.Label>
    <Field.Control type="email" />
    <Field.Error />
  </Field.Root>
  <Button type="submit">Submit</Button>
</Form>

intents.reasoning: Form's errors prop accepts a record of server-side errors keyed by field name, automatically distributing them to matching Field.Error components.
intents.composition:
<Form errors={errors} onClearErrors={handleClear} onSubmit={handleSubmit}>
  <Field.Root name="email">
    <Field.Label>Email</Field.Label>
    <Field.Control type="email" />
    <Field.Error />
  </Field.Root>
  <Button type="submit">Submit</Button>
</Form>

intents.reasoning: Input is the primary component for standalone text entry. Use when you need a simple text input without the full Field wrapper.
Input automatically integrates with Field for label, description, and validation when nested inside Field.Root.
Input works well as a simple search box. For search with suggestions, use Autocomplete instead.
Menu is the primary component for dropdown menus triggered by a button. It provides keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA attributes.
Menu supports checkbox items for toggling boolean options within a menu.
Menu supports nested submenus for organizing complex menu hierarchies.
Menubar provides a horizontal bar of menus with arrow-key navigation between them, like desktop application menu bars.
Menubar groups multiple Menu components together with shared keyboard navigation and focus management.
Meter displays a scalar measurement within a known range. Use for disk usage, battery level, password strength, etc.
Meter with destructive color communicates when a value has crossed a critical threshold.
NavigationMenu is the primary component for site-wide navigation with hover-triggered dropdown panels.
intents.composition: Products Product A Product B About

intents.reasoning: NavigationMenu content panels can hold any layout, making them ideal for mega-menu patterns with multi-column grids.
intents.composition:
Categories

Category A

Item 1

Category B

Item 1

Category C

Item 1

intents.reasoning: NumberField provides a structured numeric input with increment/decrement buttons, keyboard step support, and min/max constraints.
NumberField is ideal for quantity picking with its compact design and constrained numeric input.
Popover provides a floating panel that can contain interactive content like forms, buttons, or rich text.
Popover can host small action panels or contextual controls near their trigger.
PreviewCard shows a rich preview of content when hovering over a link or trigger.
PreviewCard is ideal for showing a summary of a user profile when hovering over their name or avatar.
Progress shows how far along a task is toward completion. Use for file uploads, form submissions, or any ongoing operation.
Progress with null value shows an indeterminate animation for operations with unknown duration.
Radio is the standard pattern for mutually exclusive choices where all options should be visible.
intents.composition:
<Radio.Group defaultValue="option1">
  <label style={{ display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', gap: 8 }}>
    <Radio.Root value="option1"><Radio.Indicator /></Radio.Root>
    Option 1
  </label>
  <label style={{ display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', gap: 8 }}>
    <Radio.Root value="option2"><Radio.Indicator /></Radio.Root>
    Option 2
  </label>
</Radio.Group>

intents.reasoning: Radio groups work well for preference or settings where users pick exactly one option from a small set.
intents.composition:
<Radio.Group defaultValue="standard">
  <label style={{ display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', gap: 8 }}>
    <Radio.Root value="standard"><Radio.Indicator /></Radio.Root>
    Standard
  </label>
  <label style={{ display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', gap: 8 }}>
    <Radio.Root value="express"><Radio.Indicator /></Radio.Root>
    Express
  </label>
</Radio.Group>

intents.reasoning: ScrollArea overlays a custom-styled scrollbar on top of native scroll, keeping all native behaviors (keyboard, touch, RTL, mouse wheel) while making the scrollbar match the design system.
ScrollArea with a horizontal Scrollbar gives a clean horizontal scrolling row (chips, tags, thumbnails) with a styled scrollbar that fades in only when needed.
Inside a Dialog or Drawer, ScrollArea provides consistent scrollbar styling that won't clash with the surrounding chrome and stays out of the way until needed.
Separator is the canonical 1px divider for splitting content groups, settings sections, or list groups.
Use orientation="vertical" inside a flex row to separate inline metadata or toolbar groups.
When the divider is purely visual and adjacent content is already grouped semantically, mark it decorative so screen readers skip it.

antiPatterns.scenario: Navigation to another page or URL
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use a link (<a> tag) or Link component instead. Buttons are for actions, links are for navigation.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use an anchor element or Link component with appropriate href.

antiPatterns.reasoning: Use a Toggle or Switch component which communicates the on/off state to assistive technology.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use Toggle from @base-ui/react/toggle or a Switch component.

antiPatterns.reasoning: Use RadioGroup, ToggleGroup, or Select instead. Buttons don't communicate selection state.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use RadioGroup, ToggleGroup, or Select components.

antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Menu.Trigger which handles aria-expanded, aria-haspopup, and keyboard navigation automatically.

antiPatterns.scenario: Switching between views or content panels
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Tabs instead. Accordion reveals content in place within the same flow, while Tabs switch between distinct views.

antiPatterns.scenario: A single collapsible section with no siblings
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Collapsible directly. Accordion is for groups of related collapsible sections. A lone collapsible section doesn't need accordion semantics.
Accordion doesn't communicate sequential progress or completion state. Use a dedicated stepper or wizard pattern.
antiPatterns.alternative: Build a custom Stepper component or use a wizard flow.

antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Dialog instead. AlertDialog is for confirmations requiring acknowledgment, not general content display.
Use Toast for transient messages. AlertDialog blocks all interaction and is too disruptive for non-critical feedback.

antiPatterns.scenario: Simple yes/no questions that are easily reversible
antiPatterns.reasoning: If the action is easily undone, an AlertDialog adds unnecessary friction. Prefer an inline undo pattern or a Toast with an undo button.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use a Toast with an undo action.

antiPatterns.scenario: A dismissible overlay that can be closed by clicking outside
antiPatterns.reasoning: AlertDialog cannot be dismissed by clicking outside — that's by design. Use Dialog if you want backdrop dismissal.
Use AlertDialog instead. Dialog can be dismissed by clicking the backdrop, which is too easy to accidentally close for critical confirmations.

antiPatterns.scenario: Non-critical transient feedback or status updates
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Toast for ephemeral messages. Dialog demands too much attention for simple feedback.

antiPatterns.scenario: Selecting from a list of options
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Select or Combobox for option selection. Dialog is overweight for a simple pick-one interaction.

antiPatterns.scenario: Content that should remain visible alongside the page
antiPatterns.reasoning: Dialog blocks the page behind an overlay. Use Collapsible or inline expansion for content that should coexist with the page.
Avatar is semantically for identity. Use a plain or a Card image for content images.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use a plain element or a Card component.

antiPatterns.reasoning: Avatar is 40px by default and circular. For large images use a dedicated hero/banner component.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use a plain with custom styling.

antiPatterns.scenario: Selectable user lists or user pickers
antiPatterns.reasoning: Avatar is presentational only. Wrap it inside a Checkbox, Radio, or Listbox item for selectable lists.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use Checkbox, Radio, or Listbox with Avatar inside.

antiPatterns.scenario: Binary on/off toggle with immediate effect (e.g., dark mode)
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Switch for settings that take effect immediately without a form submission step.

antiPatterns.scenario: Single selection from a group of mutually exclusive options
antiPatterns.reasoning: Checkboxes allow multiple selections. Use Radio for single-select groups.
Checkbox represents a state, not an action. Use Button for actions.
Use Checkbox alone. CheckboxGroup adds overhead for managing a value array that isn't needed for a single toggle.

antiPatterns.scenario: Mutually exclusive options where only one can be selected
antiPatterns.reasoning: Checkboxes allow multiple selections. Use Radio or RadioGroup for single-select.

antiPatterns.scenario: Multiple collapsible sections that belong together
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Accordion instead. Accordion manages a group of collapsible sections with proper keyboard navigation and optional single-open behavior.

antiPatterns.scenario: Switching between views or content panels
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Tabs instead. Collapsible reveals content in place; Tabs switch between distinct views.
Use Autocomplete instead. Combobox requires selection from a predefined list — the value is a selection, not free text.

antiPatterns.scenario: Short fixed list of options (5 or fewer)
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Select for small lists where search is unnecessary overhead. Combobox adds complexity with its filter input.
Use Input directly. Combobox is for selecting from options, not free-form text entry.

antiPatterns.scenario: User must select from a fixed set of options (no free text)
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Select or Combobox instead. Autocomplete allows free-form text input — the value is the text itself, not a selection.
Autocomplete does not support multiple selection. Use Combobox with multiple mode which provides Chips parts.
Use Input directly. Autocomplete adds overhead for the popup and filtering logic that is unnecessary without suggestions.
Use AlertDialog instead. Drawer is too easy to dismiss for critical confirmations.
Use Tooltip or Popover for lightweight contextual information. Drawer is overweight for a hover tip.

antiPatterns.scenario: Full-page content that needs centered modal focus
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Dialog for centered modal overlays. Drawer is anchored to an edge and slides in, which doesn't suit centered content.

antiPatterns.scenario: A standalone label without an input control
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use a plain <label> element. Field's value is in connecting label, control, and error — without a control it adds no benefit.

antiPatterns.scenario: Complex multi-input groups (e.g., date range with two inputs)
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Fieldset to group multiple related inputs. Field is for a single label-control pair.
Use Field alone. Fieldset is for grouping multiple related fields.

antiPatterns.scenario: Visual-only section dividers without semantic grouping
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use a heading or Separator for visual organization. Fieldset adds semantic grouping that affects assistive technology.
If you only need client-side validation, a plain <form> element works fine. Form adds value when you need to pipe server errors to Field components.
Form renders a <form> element. Don't use it as a generic flex-column layout container.

antiPatterns.scenario: Search input with filtered suggestion dropdown
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Autocomplete which provides the popup and filtering logic.

antiPatterns.scenario: Selecting from a predefined list of options
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Select or Combobox for option selection, not a free-text input.
Input renders a single-line <input>. Use a <textarea> or Field.Control with render prop for multi-line.

antiPatterns.scenario: Navigation links that should be visible at all times
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use a visible navigation bar or NavigationMenu instead. Menus hide their content until triggered.
antiPatterns.alternative: NavigationMenu or plain nav with links

antiPatterns.scenario: Selecting a single value from a list (form input)
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Select or Combobox for form value selection. Menu is for actions, not form values.
Use ContextMenu which handles the right-click trigger pattern. Menu requires an explicit trigger button.
Use Menu alone. Menubar adds keyboard navigation between multiple menus that isn't needed for a single menu.
Use NavigationMenu which is designed for site navigation with hover-triggered content panels.
Use Toolbar which is designed for groups of action buttons and controls, not menus.

antiPatterns.scenario: Showing progress of a task that will complete
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Progress for indeterminate or determinate task progress. Meter is for static scalar values, not ongoing operations.
Use Progress with indeterminate mode or a spinner. Meter represents a measurement, not a loading state.
Meter shows a continuous bar. For discrete steps, use a rating component or radio group.

antiPatterns.scenario: Action menus triggered by a button click
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Menu for action dropdowns. NavigationMenu is for site navigation with hover-triggered content.
Use Drawer for mobile slide-out navigation. NavigationMenu is designed for desktop hover interactions.
Use Menubar with Menu components. NavigationMenu is for site navigation, not application commands.
Use Input for general text entry. NumberField is specifically for numeric values with step controls.

antiPatterns.scenario: Selecting a value within a continuous range
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Slider for visual range selection. NumberField is better for precise numeric entry.

antiPatterns.scenario: Phone numbers, credit cards, or formatted numeric strings
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Input with appropriate pattern/mask. NumberField treats values as numbers, not formatted strings.
Use Tooltip for non-interactive hover text. Popover is for content that users interact with.
Use Dialog for modal workflows. Popover is for lightweight contextual content.
Use NavigationMenu or Menu for structured navigation. Popover is for arbitrary content.
Use Popover for content with buttons, forms, or interactive elements. PreviewCard is for passive previews.
Use NavigationMenu for structured navigation with links and sections.
Use Tooltip for brief, non-interactive labels or descriptions.

antiPatterns.scenario: Showing a scalar measurement within a range
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use Meter for static values like disk usage or battery level. Progress is for ongoing operations.
Use a spinner component for compact loading states. Progress is a horizontal bar.
Use a stepper component for step-by-step progress. Progress shows continuous completion.
Use CheckboxGroup when users can select more than one option.
Use Select or Combobox for long lists to save space and improve usability.
Use Switch for a single boolean toggle. Radio is for choosing between labeled options.

antiPatterns.scenario: The whole document or viewport scrolls
antiPatterns.reasoning: Use the browser's native scroll for full-page scrolling. ScrollArea is for a bounded region; using it on the document hijacks page scroll and breaks anchor links and accessibility heuristics.

antiPatterns.scenario: Virtualized lists with thousands of items
antiPatterns.reasoning: ScrollArea wraps native scroll but doesn't virtualize. For very large lists, pair it with a virtualization library (TanStack Virtual, react-window) or skip it entirely — the custom scrollbar may interfere with item measurement.

antiPatterns.scenario: Content that should grow with the page
antiPatterns.reasoning: ScrollArea is for fixed-size regions. If the container should expand to fit content, just use a regular div — no scrollbar is needed.

antiPatterns.scenario: Replacing a heading or section landmark
antiPatterns.reasoning: Separator is decoration, not structure. Use a heading or landmark for semantic grouping.

antiPatterns.scenario: As a card or panel border
antiPatterns.reasoning: Containers should own their borders. Adding a Separator inside duplicates the visual.
Those components ship their own Separator part wired to their roving focus and ARIA model.
